edges.description: MEFV mutations cause inflammasome activation and uncontrolled IL-1β production, resulting in recurrent attacks of fever and polyserositis.
Pyrin inflammasome activation and excess IL-1 beta drive recurrent inflammatory fever attacks.
Autoinflammatory attacks involve serosal inflammation of the peritoneum.
Autoinflammatory attacks involve serosal inflammation of the pleura.
Autoinflammatory attacks can involve serosal inflammation of the pericardium.
Pyrin-driven inflammatory attacks can include arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Arthritis in familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: The arthritis was transient, monoarticular, nonerosive, and nondeforming in the majority of cases. Four patients (5.4%) had chronic arthritis, with one requiring total hip replacement. As in previous reports on arthritis of FMF, the majority of FMF patients studied in Lebanon had a transient monoarticular nonerosive and nondeforming type of arthritis affecting predominantly the large joints of the lower extremities.

evidence.reference_title: Erysipelas-like erythema in children with familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: More patients had arthritis in the ELE group than in the other group (p=0.011). Arthritis occurred in the ankle (77.4%), knee (19.3%) and hip (3.2%) joints.

nodes.description: Dysregulated inflammatory response due to pyrin dysfunction, leading to recurrent inflammatory episodes.

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever and the other autoinflammatory syndromes: evaluation of the patient with recurrent fever.
evidence.snippet: The spectrum of reported genetic mutations and susceptible ethnicities for the hereditary periodic fever subset of the autoinflammatory diseases has continued to expand. At the same time, the pathogeneses of many of these diseases are now understood to involve different aspects of a common pathway, largely affecting inflammatory cascades related to IL-1 or tumor necrosis factor-alpha.

evidence.reference_title: Function and mechanism of the pyrin inflammasome.
evidence.snippet: Pyrin, encoded by the MEFV gene, is an intracellular pattern recognition receptor that assembles inflammasome complexes in response to pathogen infections. Mutations in the MEFV gene have been linked to autoinflammatory diseases such as familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) or pyrin-associated autoinflammation with neutrophilic dermatosis (PAAND).

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever: the molecular pathways from stress exposure to attacks.
evidence.snippet: FMF is an autoinflammatory disease characterized by recurrent attacks and increased IL-1 synthesis owing to activation of the pyrin inflammasome

evidence.reference_title: Ancient familial Mediterranean fever mutations in human pyrin and resistance to Yersinia pestis.
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is an autoinflammatory disease caused by homozygous or compound heterozygous gain-of-function mutations in MEFV, which encodes pyrin, an inflammasome protein. Heterozygous carrier frequencies for multiple MEFV mutations are high in several Mediterranean populations, suggesting that they confer selective advantage.

evidence.reference_title: Cardiac disease in familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is an autoinflammatory disease manifested by inflammatory attacks of peritonitis, pleuritis, pericarditis accompanied by fever and arthritis. Mutations of MEFV gene results in pyrin dysfunction, which causes uncontrolled interleukin-1 beta production and triggers the inflammatory attacks.

evidence.reference_title: [Role of genetics in familial Mediterranean fever].
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is caused by mutations within the Mediterranean fever (MEFV) gene. These gain of function mutations lead to an increased activation of the inflammasome pyrin with a subsequent disproportional proinflammatory reaction.

evidence.reference_title: Immunological function of familial Mediterranean fever disease protein Pyrin.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the MEFV gene are associated with the human autoinflammatory disease familial Mediterranean fever (FMF). Pyrin can interact with the inflammasome adaptor ASC and induce inflammatory caspase-1 activation in monocytic cells.

evidence.reference_title: MEFV gene mutation spectrum in patients with familial mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: MEFV gene mutation spectrum in patients with familial mediterranean fever

evidence.reference_title: MEFV / familial Mediterranean fever (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: MEFV | HGNC:6998 | familial Mediterranean fever | MONDO:0018088 | SD | Definitive

nodes.description: Mutations in the MEFV gene lead to dysfunctional pyrin, causing excessive inflammation.

evidence.reference_title: [Role of genetics in familial Mediterranean fever].
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is caused by mutations within the Mediterranean fever (MEFV) gene. These gain of function mutations lead to an increased activation of the inflammasome pyrin with a subsequent disproportional proinflammatory reaction.

evidence.reference_title: Immunological function of familial Mediterranean fever disease protein Pyrin.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the MEFV gene are associated with the human autoinflammatory disease familial Mediterranean fever (FMF). Pyrin can interact with the inflammasome adaptor ASC and induce inflammatory caspase-1 activation in monocytic cells.

evidence.snippet: MEFV gene mutation spectrum in patients with familial mediterranean fever

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever: a differential diagnosis for the surgical abdomen.
evidence.snippet: FMF is characterised by dysregulation of the inflammatory process in the body, presenting as recurrent episodes of serositis. Patients with FMF commonly present with episodes of fever, peritonitis, synovitis, pleuritis, arthritis, and occasionally pericarditis.

evidence.reference_title: Evaluation of Gastrointestinal System Complaints and Comorbidities in Pediatric Familial Mediterranean Fever Patients.
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is the most prevalent hereditary autoinflammatory disease among children. Abdominal pain and various gastrointestinal system (GIS) manifestations may arise directly from FMF or concomitantly with FMF.

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever: the genetics of inflammation.
evidence.snippet: When a patient complains of episodic fever accompanied by unexplained arthritis, peritonitis, pleurisy, or skin rash, this disorder should be considered.

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever may mimic acute appendicitis in children.
evidence.snippet: The key feature of familial Mediterranean fever is relapsing episodes of fever and serositis including peritonitis, pleurisy, or arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean Fever with Small Bowel Stenosis.
evidence.snippet: Considering the history of recurrent abdominal pain, Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) was considered.

evidence.reference_title: Chest pain in children with familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: The most common signs and symptoms are fever, abdominal pain, chest pain, and arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Thoracic and lung involvement in familial Mediterranean fever (FMF).
evidence.snippet: Lung involvement in FMF is limited mainly to transient pleuritis during acute attacks.

evidence.reference_title: Transient and Recurrent Pulmonary Infiltrations Associated with Familial Mediterranean Fever.
evidence.snippet: Pericarditis, pleurisy and the response to colchicine indicated FMF.

evidence.reference_title: Apical fibrosis was the most common incidental pulmonary finding in a familial Mediterranean fever cohort.
evidence.snippet: Pleuritis is the only known pulmonary involvement of FMF; however, as far as we know, thoracic involvements in pleural, parenchymal, bronchial, and vascular structures have not been evaluated yet.

evidence.reference_title: Familial Mediterranean fever--a review.
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever type 1 is characterized by recurrent short episodes of inflammation and serositis, including fever, peritonitis, synovitis, pleuritis, and, rarely, pericarditis.

evidence.reference_title: The myths we believed in familial Mediterranean fever: what have we learned in the past years?
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever...presents with recurrent and self-limited inflammatory attacks of fever and polyserositis along with high acute-phase reactants.

evidence.reference_title: Disease severity and genotype-phenotype correlation in adult patients with familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: Higher Pras scores, earlier age of symptoms and diagnosis, more frequent arthritis and erysipelas-like erythema, and higher colchicine dose are closely associated with M694V homozygous familial Mediterranean fever patients. These patients also have mostly moderate and severe disease severity.

evidence.reference_title: The analysis of genotype-phenotype correlation in familial Mediterranean fever.
evidence.snippet: Familial Mediterranean fever (FMF) is the most common monogenic autoinflammatory disease manifesting with phenotypic heterogeneity. It is a clinically diagnosed disease supported by Mediterranean fever gene mutation analysis.
